title: Carrier-density dependence of magnetotransport in correlated Dirac semimetal CaIrO$_3$
authors: Rinsuke Yamada, Jun Fujioka, Minoru Kawamura, Tatsuya Okawa, Yoshio Kaneko, Shiro Sakai, Motoaki Hirayama, Ryotaro Arita, Kiyohiro Adachi, Daisuke Hashizume, Yoshinori Tokura
abstract: We report the carrier density dependence of the magnetotransport property in the correlated Dirac semimetal CaIrO$_3$. In the dilute carrier density region ($n_{\rm H}$ $\sim 2.2 \times 10^{16} \,$$\rm{cm}^{-3}$) at $2 \, \mathrm{K}$, the mobility exceeds $1.0 \times 10^{5} \,$$\rm{cm}^{2}/\rm{Vs}$ at $2 \, \mathrm{K}$, and the transverse magnetoresistance (MR) reaches $2,000 \,$\% at $12 \, \mathrm{T}$. The analysis of quantum oscillations and Hall conductivity shows that the Fermi velocity is nearly independent of the cross-sectional area of the Fermi surface, or equivalently the carrier density, supporting a $k$-linear dispersion of the Dirac node. The field dependence of magnetoresistivity is nearly $B$-linear in the moderate carrier density region ($n_\mathrm{H} \geq 4 \times 10^{16}\,$cm$^{-3}$), but scales with $B^α$ ($α> 2$) in the lower carrier density region. The variation of magnetoresistivity is likely affected by the enhanced long-range Coulomb interaction in the quantum limit, where Dirac electrons are subject to the magnetic confinement.
summary: This paper explores how changing the carrier density in the Dirac semimetal CaIrO3 affects its transport properties. It demonstrates that at low densities, the system enters a quantum limit where enhanced Coulomb interactions lead to non-linear magnetoresistance. The results confirm the presence of a robust Dirac dispersion characterized by a constant Fermi velocity.
main_problem: Investigating the carrier-density dependence of magnetotransport properties, specifically the origin of giant magnetoresistance and the transition in MR scaling behavior in the correlated Dirac semimetal CaIrO3.
main_result: The study finds that as carrier density decreases, mobility and magnetoresistance increase, with the MR scaling transitioning from B-linear to B-nonlinear (alpha > 2) due to enhanced long-range Coulomb interactions in the quantum limit. The constant Fermi velocity across different carrier densities provides strong evidence for k-linear Dirac dispersion.
method: Transport measurements including resistivity, Hall effect, and Shubnikov-de Haas oscillations were performed on single crystals at low temperatures and high magnetic fields, supplemented by DFT+DMFT modeling.
model_or_system: The system is CaIrO3, an orthorhombic perovskite characterized as a correlated Dirac semimetal with a line node in its energy dispersion and 5d orbitals featuring a j_eff = 1/2 multiplet.
key_observables: Carrier density (nH), mobility, transverse magnetoresistance (MR) ratio, effective mass (mc), Fermi velocity (vF), and MR exponent (alpha).
important_parameters: Low carrier density regime (nH ~ 2.2 x 10^16 cm^-3), quantum limit, magnetic fields up to 14 T, and temperatures down to 2 K.
main_assumptions: Difficulty in precisely estimating the Thomas-Fermi screening length and the exact strength of the impurity potential.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the crystal structure, energy dispersion schematic, and temperature/density dependence of resistivity and MR; Fig 2 compares Hall resistivity and MR ratio across samples; Fig 3 verifies Kohler's rule.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the material and its electronic structure, presents transport data across varying carrier densities, analyzes quantum oscillations to characterize the Dirac dispersion, discusses the scaling of magnetoresistance, and concludes with a physical interpretation of the scattering mechanisms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
